examples.title: Simple SYN Flood Attack Simulation
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating how a SYN flood attack works
examples.code:
ALGORITHM SYN_Flood
INPUT: targetIP, targetPort, numPackets
BEGIN
  FOR i = 1 TO numPackets DO
    // Create TCP socket
    sock = CreateSocket(TCP)
    // Generate random source IP (spoofed)
    srcIP = GenerateRandomIP()
    // Send SYN packet (half-open connection)
    SendPacket(sock, srcIP, targetIP, targetPort, SYN flag)
    // Do NOT complete handshake (no ACK)
    CloseSocket(sock)
  END FOR
END
examples.explanation: This demonstrates the SYN flood principle: sending many SYN requests without completing the TCP handshake, creating half-open connections that exhaust server resources.

examples.title: Simple DDoS Detection Script
examples.description: Python code to detect potential DDoS attack by monitoring connection rates
examples.code:
import socket
import time
from collections import defaultdict

class DDoSDetector:
    def __init__(self, threshold=100, time_window=10):
        self.threshold = threshold
        self.time_window = time_window
        self.connection_counts = defaultdict(list)
    
    def monitor_connection(self, ip_address):
        current_time = time.time()
        # Remove old connections outside time window
        self.connection_counts[ip_address] = [
            t for t in self.connection_counts[ip_address]
            if current_time - t < self.time_window
        ]
        # Add current connection
        self.connection_counts[ip_address].append(current_time)
        
        # Check if threshold exceeded
        if len(self.connection_counts[ip_address]) > self.threshold:
            return True  # Potential DDoS attack detected
        return False

# Usage
detector = DDoSDetector(threshold=50, time_window=10)
test_ips = ['192.168.1.1'] * 60

for ip in test_ips:
    if detector.monitor_connection(ip):
        print(f"ALERT: Possible DDoS attack from {ip}")
        break
examples.explanation: This script monitors incoming connections and flags potential DDoS attacks when a single IP exceeds the connection threshold within a time window.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(m) where m is unique IPs

examples.title: Rate Limiting Implementation
examples.description: C code demonstrating simple rate limiting using token bucket algorithm
examples.explanation: This demonstrates the token bucket rate limiting algorithm, commonly used to mitigate DDoS attacks by restricting the number of requests a client can make within a time period.

examples.title: DDoS Mitigation Workflow
examples.description: Workflow for detecting and mitigating DDoS attacks
examples.code:
ALGORITHM DDoS_Mitigation
BEGIN
  INITIALIZE traffic_monitor
  INITIALIZE blacklist = empty
  INITIALIZE whitelist = trusted_ips
  
  WHILE system_active DO
    incoming_traffic = capture_traffic()
    
    // Analyze traffic patterns
    FOR each packet IN incoming_traffic DO
      src_ip = packet.source_ip
      
      IF src_ip IN whitelist THEN
        ALLOW packet
      ELSE IF src_ip IN blacklist THEN
        DROP packet
      ELSE
        // Check for attack patterns
        IF is_anomalous(packet) THEN
          increment_threat_score(src_ip)
          IF threat_score(src_ip) > THRESHOLD THEN
            ADD src_ip TO blacklist
            LOG "IP blocked: " + src_ip
          END IF
        ELSE
          ALLOW packet
        END IF
      END IF
    END FOR
    
    // Clean up old blacklist entries periodically
    IF time_elapsed > cleanup_interval THEN
      remove_expired_blacklist_entries()
    END IF
  END WHILE
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows a typical DDoS mitigation workflow that monitors traffic, identifies anomalous patterns, and blocks IPs that exceed threat thresholds.
